saida_bruta:
There was nothing imposing or giant-like about the man gradually awakening on the sleeping porch of a Dutch Colonial house in Floral Heights, a quiet neighborhood in the city of Zenith.

His name was George F. Babbitt. In April of 1920, he was forty-six years old. He did not manufacture anything—neither butter, shoes, nor poetry—but he excelled at the business of selling houses to people who could barely afford them.

Babbitt’s head was large and pink, his brown hair thin and dry. In sleep, his face appeared almost childlike, despite the wrinkles and the indentations left by his spectacles on the bridge of his nose. He was not overweight, but clearly well nourished; his cheeks were plump, and the smooth, uncalloused hand resting on the khaki blanket was slightly swollen. He seemed prosperous, settled into a comfortable and unremarkable married life, and thoroughly practical. The sleeping porch reflected this same spirit: it overlooked a broad elm, two tidy lawns, a concrete driveway, and a corrugated iron garage—nothing especially romantic. Yet, as before, Babbitt was dreaming of a fairy child, a vision more enchanting than pagodas gleaming by a silver sea.

Babbitt’s head was large and pink, his brown hair sparse and dry. While asleep, his face looked almost youthful, despite its wrinkles and the marks left on his nose by his glasses. He was not fat, but his well-fed cheeks and the smooth, uncalloused hand lying on the khaki blanket revealed a life of comfort. He radiated prosperity and the quiet contentment of a settled, practical man. The porch itself mirrored his character: it offered a view of a broad elm, two neat lawns, a concrete driveway, and a corrugated-iron garage—nothing particularly picturesque. Still, as so often before, Babbitt was dreaming of a fairy child, a vision more magical than pagodas shining beside a silvery sea.

He radiated prosperity and the quiet confidence of a practical, established businessman. The porch suited him precisely: it overlooked a large elm, two tidy lawns, a concrete driveway, and a corrugated iron garage—nothing especially scenic. Yet, as so often before, Babbitt drifted into dreams of a fairy child, an enchanting vision far more compelling than pagodas shining on some distant, silvered shore.
